- The project repo is mounted at the burrow workspace root.
- /workspace/.canopy/agent.json is the rendered agent definition (warren seeded it).

Operating contract:
- Edit files in place. Run tests when relevant.
- Quality gates are terminal, not advisory. You are NOT done until the gate exits zero. Resolve the command in this order: `$WARREN_QUALITY_GATE` if set, otherwise the command documented in CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md, otherwise fall back to `bun run check:all` or `npm run lint && npm run typecheck && npm test`. Run it before committing and again before reporting completion. Do not declare the task complete, hand off, or end the session with a red gate — fix failures (including lint warnings, which CI treats as errors) until it is green. If the gate is genuinely unfixable in this run, say so explicitly and leave the work open rather than claiming success.
- Use git as you normally would. Commit your changes; warren reaps the branch and pushes upstream.
- Committing is mandatory, not the same as staging. `git add` ALONE IS NOT ENOUGH — you must run `git commit` so the work lands as a real commit. A run that ends with staged-but-uncommitted changes is treated as a FAILURE (`dropped_commit`), not a success. Before you report completion, run `git status`/`git log` and confirm your changes are in a commit, not just staged. The only exception is when you have genuinely made no file changes at all.
- Do not run `git push` yourself — warren handles the push host-side after the run terminates.

{"id":"warren-0d2d","title":"Agents don't reliably close their seed when prompted to","status":"open","type":"bug","priority":3,"createdAt":"2026-06-05T17:10:22.522Z","updatedAt":"2026-06-06T21:39:24.464Z","description":"<!-- seeds:plan-backref:start -->

Repro (run_e7q2a63jrc4z, 2026-06-05): dispatch prompt explicitly said 'Open a PR and close the seed when done'. The agent (claude-code, builtin) shipped the fix, opened PR #1 (jayminwest/kota-voice), committed, but left seed kota-voice-0abf in 'open' state. The operator (KOTA) had to pull and 'sd close' by hand after the merge.

Hypotheses to confirm:
1. The 'sd' binary isn't installed in the burrow image for the claude-code runtime, so the close step silently no-ops or the agent skips it.
2. 'sd' is present but auth/git config in the burrow means 'sd close' + 'sd sync' don't round-trip back through the PR — the close happens on the run branch and is reaped, but the merge of the PR doesn't carry the .seeds/ change because the close came after the commit/push step in the agent's flow.
3. The agent's system prompt biases it toward 'finish at the PR', treating seed bookkeeping as out of scope.

Fix direction (pick one or stack):
- Make seed-closing a *runtime* concern, not an agent concern: on successful reap with a non-null seedId, Warren itself runs 'sd close <seed>' against the project (host-side, after merge) and commits a 'seeds: sync' on main. Removes the burden from every agent and every prompt.
- Or: ensure 'sd' is on PATH in every builtin runtime image and add it to the operating contract in the rendered system prompt ('If the prompt names a seed_id, you MUST sd close it before reporting completion').
- Or both — runtime as the safety net, agent as the happy path.

Acceptance:
- A successful run with a seedId leaves that seed closed in main once the PR is merged, without operator intervention.
- The close shows up in git history as either an agent commit on the PR branch or a Warren-host commit on main, never silently dropped.","labels":["agent","seeds","dispatch"],"plan_id":"pl-247b","blocks":["warren-6daa","warren-7cea"]}

Nothing reaped it: burrow run stayed 'running', exitCode null, and warren happily showed it live. There is no per-tool/per-bash or wall-clock budget that force-terminates a stuck command or fails the run. agent.pauseTimeoutMs only covers paused/question turns, not a busy-but-silent tool. Add a configurable command/heartbeat timeout (or surface burrow's run timeout) so a hung gate command reaps the run to failed instead of hanging forever. Repro evidence: process tree on warren-deployed showed bash->bun->node biome->biome cli accumulating ~57 CPU-min; killing the biome pid let the agent resume (and it immediately re-ran the same gate and re-hung — deterministic).

UPDATE (cancel does not reap the sandbox process tree): POST /runs/run_wawgn5tbejkx/cancel returned {state:cancelled, alreadyTerminal:false} and the run row went state=cancelled/ended_at set, but the runaway in-sandbox process tree (bash -> bun run lint:fix -> node biome -> biome cli) kept running and kept pinning ~4 cores until I manually 'kill -9'd the pids on the machine. So cancel marks the warren run terminal without terminating the burrow run's child process tree — same class of leak as the missing gate timeout. Routing through reap's workspace_destroy tears down the burrow workspace so a hung gate command no longer wedges the run forever. Note: the residual 'cancel/destroy must actually kill the in-sandbox bwrap process tree' is a burrow-side guarantee (DELETE /burrows/:id semantics) — warren now reliably issues the destroy; if orphaned children survive it, file against burrow."}

content.text:
/**
 * `reapRun` — SPEC §4.3 step 6 + §11.A.
 *
 * Once burrow says a run reached a terminal state, warren runs reap to
 * close out the run. Best-effort sub-steps run in order: mulch merge,
 * seeds-close mirror, plans mirror, plot merge, plot commit, seeds
 * commit, auto-plan-run dispatch, branch push, PR open, preview
 * launch, PR preview annotation. Then the warren run row transitions
 * to the burrow-observed outcome.
 *
 * Each sub-step lives in its own module under `src/runs/reap/`. See:
 *
 *   - `mulch.ts`        — mulch-expertise LWW merge
 *   - `seeds.ts`        — seeds close mirror + plans mirror
 *   - `plot-merge.ts`   — Plot events + state replay
 *   - `stage.ts`        — `.plot/` + `.seeds/` commit-through-reap
 *   - `auto-plan-run.ts`— auto-dispatch helper for agent-created plans
 *   - `pr-open.ts`      — auto-open PR (warren-f6af)
 *   - `preview.ts`      — preview launch + PR annotation (warren-f156)
 *   - `state.ts`        — failure-reason inference + terminal transition
 *   - `run.ts`          — top-level orchestrator
 *
 * Reap errors never fail the run — each sub-step is wrapped, and any
 * thrown error is recorded as a `reap_failed` event on the run with
 * the failing step name. The state transition still runs regardless,
 * so a reap failure cannot leave the warren row stuck in `running`.
 *
 * Idempotent: calling `reapRun` against a row already in a terminal
 * state is a no-op — useful for restart-recovery sweeps that re-issue
 * reap for runs that finalized in burrow while warren was offline.
 *
 * Every observable side effect (file IO, git push, system clock,
 * burrow client) is injectable so unit tests don't touch disk or shell.
 */

Add a safety net that closes the dispatched run's associated seed
after a successful reap, even if the agent didn't call \`sd close\`.

  in the project clone path and emits seeds.seed_id_closed event

  add seedIdClosed: boolean to ReapRunResult
- src/runs/reap/run.ts: wire closeRunSeedId after mirrorSeeds/
  mirrorPlans and before stageSeedsForCommit so the close lands in
  the chore(warren): seeds state commit on the branch
- src/runs/reap/test-helpers.ts, util.ts, watchdog.test.ts:
  add seedIdClosed: false to result shapes
- src/runs/reap/seeds.test.ts: 5 new tests covering close success,
  no-seedId skip, failed-outcome skip, sd-error surfaces as
  reap_failed (best-effort), and no-seedsCli skip
- scripts/file-size-budgets.json: grandfather run.ts at 527 lines

project.hasSeeds==true, seedsCli configured. Failure is
best-effort (reap_failed step=seed_id_close, run still succeeds).

and sd close is a no-op."
